author: TX Labs, LC, CoreNest Capital Limited, and Texture Capital, Inc.
key_points: Representatives of tx (TX Labs), CoreNest Capital, and Texture Capital met with Crypto Task Force staff on May 19, 2026, to discuss regulatory approaches to crypto assets and tokenized real-world assets.

The participants described tx as a Layer-1 blockchain for tokenized RWAs, with regulated activities conducted through affiliates such as Texture Capital, which they said has FINRA approval to issue SoloTex Stock Tokens.

The participants highlighted 'Smart Token' functionality embedding compliance controls (whitelisting, transfer restrictions, freeze/clawback) at the token level, rather than relying solely on smart contracts or third parties.

The participants expressed interest in the SEC's Innovation Exemption, clarity for non-custodial interfaces, and pathways to compliant decentralized secondary-market trading of tokenized assets currently limited to primary issuance and redemption.
takeaway: TX Labs and affiliates urged the SEC to enable compliant secondary trading of tokenized RWAs using embedded compliance controls and sought Innovation Exemption clarity.

title: Meeting with Copper Technologies (UK) Limited and Sobba Public, LLC
author: Copper Technologies (UK) Limited and Sobba Public, LLC
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met on May 13, 2026 with Amar Kuchinad of Copper Technologies (UK) Limited and Alan Sobba of Sobba Public, LLC to discuss regulatory approaches to crypto assets.

Copper described itself as B2B market infrastructure for institutional custody, settlement, and collateral mobility of blockchain-based assets, highlighting its ClearLoop Network for pledging and moving derivatives collateral.

The presenters raised questions about which regulators would oversee purchase/sale permutations (digital securities, digital commodities, fiat, stablecoins) and how capital requirements would apply across DvP, DvD, and FoP/FoD settlement.

The presenters asked whether SEC-regulated clearinghouses and security-based swap dealers may accept blockchain-based assets as initial and variation margin, and sought clarification on the April 13, 2026 staff statement regarding Covered User Interface Providers, qualified custodians, and financing arrangements.
takeaway: Copper Technologies and Sobba Public discussed institutional custody, settlement, and collateral mobility regulation with the SEC Crypto Task Force.

title: Meeting with Wall Street Blockchain Alliance
author: Wall Street Blockchain Alliance
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met with Wall Street Blockchain Alliance (WSBA) representatives on May 13, 2026 to discuss approaches to regulating crypto assets, based on an agenda document provided by WSBA.

The proposed agenda indicated WSBA planned to share perspectives on recent SEC interpretive guidance, SEC-CFTC coordination, and institutional compliance considerations affecting U.S. market competitiveness.

WSBA's agenda covered digital asset market structure topics including ETFs, tokenization, broker-dealer and custody frameworks, tokenized securities, and market infrastructure such as settlement and interoperability.

Additional agenda items included payment stablecoins in regulated markets, registration pathways for intermediaries, distinguishing securities from non-securities, and WSBA priorities for further SEC guidance and structured industry engagement.
takeaway: WSBA met with SEC Crypto Task Force to discuss broad digital asset market structure topics including tokenization, custody, stablecoins, and regulatory frameworks.

title: Meeting with sFOX Inc.
author: sFOX Inc.
key_points: sFOX representatives met with Crypto Task Force staff on May 12, 2026 to discuss a proposed two-phase pilot for institutional secondary-market trading of tokenized real-world assets under an Innovation Exemption framework.

sFOX described Phase 1 as tokenized commodities (e.g., gold, silver) using its existing Wyoming trust and FinCEN MSB authorities, and Phase 2 as tokenized securities subject to federal securities laws and exemption conditions.
sFOX stated it intends to operate as a trading venue and intermediary rather than an issuer or originator, partnering with third-party tokenization platforms and serving institutional clients only.
sFOX sought staff feedback on guardrails, SEC-CFTC jurisdictional harmonization, and whether the pilot should proceed via registration, exemptive relief, or no-action assurances.
takeaway: sFOX proposed a two-phase pilot for institutional trading of tokenized real-world assets, seeking SEC guidance on exemptive relief and cross-agency jurisdictional coordination.

title: Meeting with BSV Association, 3 Hare Court Chambers, Cozen O’Connor Public Strategies, LLC
author: BSV Association, 3 Hare Court Chambers, Cozen O’Connor Public Strategies, LLC
key_points: BSV Association representatives, joined by advisors from 3 Hare Court Chambers and Cozen O'Connor Public Strategies, met with Crypto Task Force staff on May 11, 2026 to discuss regulation of crypto assets.

The requestors stated their purpose was to provide technical and governance context—not to seek relief or exemptions—regarding how BSV's utility-based design interacts with the SEC's recent token taxonomy guidance.

The requestors proposed to characterize BSV as scalable data/transaction infrastructure, describe the BSV Association as a standards body rather than issuer, and highlight objective, non-discretionary network access rules.
The requestors suggested the taxonomy guidance may benefit from further clarification as applied to utility-first blockchains, distinguishing native-token technical use from investment-contract use.
takeaway: BSV Association met with SEC Crypto Task Force to argue BSV's utility-based design warrants distinct treatment under the SEC's token taxonomy guidance.

title: Meeting with European Fund and Asset Management Association, The Investment Association, BNP Paribas Asset Management, Irish Funds, Asset Management Association Switzerland, and Generali Investments Holding S.p.A.
author: European Fund and Asset Management Association, The Investment Association, BNP Paribas Asset Management, Irish Funds, Asset Management Association Switzerland, and Generali Investments Holding S.p.A.
key_points: EFAMA and co-participants met with Crypto Task Force staff on April 30, 2026 to discuss crypto asset regulation, tokenization market developments, the US Clarity Act, the SEC's January statement on tokenized securities, and EU frameworks including MiCA and the DLT Pilot Regime.

EFAMA's shared report projected tokenized asset market growth of roughly 20% annually to €2 trillion by 2030, and characterized digital assets as no longer experimental within European asset management.

EFAMA recommended raising DLT Pilot Regime thresholds, harmonizing national digital asset custody rules, aligning UCITS/AIFMD with MiCA, and permitting UCITS to hold MiCA-compliant e-money tokens as liquid payment instruments.

EFAMA stated it is neutral on cash-on-chain solutions, favoring coexistence of a digital euro, MiCA-regulated stablecoins, and CBMTs, and urged an industry-agreed digital asset taxonomy and clarifications supporting tokenized MMFs as collateral.
takeaway: European asset management groups urged SEC Crypto Task Force to harmonize tokenization rules, raise DLT thresholds, and align EU-US regulatory frameworks.

title: Meeting with Presume, Inc. and Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman LLP
author: Presume, Inc. and Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman LLP
key_points: Presume, Inc. and Pillsbury Winthrop Shaw Pittman met with Crypto Task Force staff on April 29, 2026 to discuss regulatory approaches to Presume's Programmable Interest Tokens (PITs).

Presume noted that its counsel submitted a March 30, 2026 no-action request to the Division of Corporation Finance, arguing PITs qualify as 'digital tools' under the Commission's March 17, 2026 interpretive release rather than securities.

Presume represented that PITs function as interoperable loyalty rewards redeemable for Partner discounts subject to a Margin Cap, confer no equity, revenue, yield, or profit rights, and are issued via a non-discretionary formula.
Presume expressed continued interest in Commissioner Peirce's Safe Harbor concept and offered to participate in any pilot programs or testbed initiatives for early-stage crypto companies.
takeaway: Presume sought Task Force guidance arguing its Programmable Interest Tokens are digital tools, not securities, and referenced a pending no-action request.

title: Meeting with StratX LLC
author: StratX LLC
key_points: StratX representatives described their platform as a non-custodial, non-executing supervisory validation layer and asked the Task Force to confirm whether it falls outside broker-dealer, exchange, or ATS registration requirements.

StratX presented a pre-execution compliance model with fail-closed defaults and institution-authored policy overlays, asserting this approach shifts oversight from post-hoc enforcement to preventative validation.
StratX stated its audit outputs use ISO 20022, ISO 8601, ISO 4217, GLEIF LEI, and XBRL-compatible formats, and asked whether these satisfy Commission ingestion and evidentiary expectations.

StratX indicated it is not seeking registration, exemption, or no-action relief, but dialogue on classification, potential sandbox or pilot use, and cross-agency (SEC/CFTC) coordination through versioned policy updates.
takeaway: StratX asked the SEC Crypto Task Force to clarify whether its non-custodial, pre-execution compliance validation layer falls outside existing registration requirements.

title: Meeting with Etherealize, Inc.
author: Etherealize, Inc.
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met on April 24, 2026 with Etherealize representatives Vivek Raman, Danny Ryan, and Steve Yelderman to discuss approaches to crypto asset regulation.

Etherealize sought discussion on applying existing frameworks (Regulation D, Rule 144A, Regulation S) to tokenized fixed-income and real-world assets issued on the Ethereum blockchain using compliance-aware token standards.

Etherealize raised regulatory considerations for on-chain utility of tokenized securities, including collateralization, lending, and automated settlement via smart contracts interacting with decentralized finance protocols.

Etherealize expressed interest in discussing zero-knowledge cryptography for privacy-preserving compliance and areas where additional Commission guidance would facilitate institutional participation in on-chain markets.
takeaway: Etherealize discussed applying existing securities exemptions to tokenized real-world assets on Ethereum, including DeFi utility and privacy-preserving compliance.

title: Meeting with Ledger and Thorn Run Partners LLC
author: Ledger and Thorn Run Partners LLC
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met on April 22, 2026 with representatives of Ledger and Thorn Run Partners LLC to discuss approaches to crypto asset regulation, referencing an attached letter dated April 6, 2026.

Ledger's representatives requested discussion of how self-custodial wallets and wallet software front-end interfaces should be treated under broker-dealer and exchange registration requirements of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934.
Ledger's representatives also sought to discuss enabling self-custody by retail and institutional customers for crypto asset securities, tokenized securities, and funds.
Attendees for Ledger included Seth Hertlein and Reuben Smith-Vaughan, joined by Chris Hayes of Thorn Run Partners.
takeaway: Ledger met with SEC Crypto Task Force to discuss regulatory treatment of self-custodial wallets under broker-dealer and exchange registration requirements.

title: Meeting with Octet, Variant Fund, and Arktouros PLLC
author: Octet, Variant Fund, and Arktouros PLLC
key_points: Representatives of Octet, Variant Fund, and Arktouros PLLC met with Crypto Task Force staff on April 21, 2026 to discuss approaches to crypto asset regulation, submitting a written agenda for discussion.

Octet's representatives argued that securities law, citing Reg S and Morrison v. National Australia Bank, treats transaction location as dispositive, and contended KYC does not verify a party's location at the moment of an onchain transaction.

The submission asserted that the Commission's March 2026 Joint Interpretation confirms sanctions screening obligations apply to tokenized instruments, and urged continuous jurisdictional verification before onchain settlement.

Octet described its device-level cryptographic proof of physical presence and asked how such privacy-preserving location proofs might fit Reg S safe harbor, sanctions screening, and tokenized securities compliance.
takeaway: Octet, Variant Fund, and Arktouros urged the Task Force to consider device-level location proofs for Reg S compliance and sanctions screening of tokenized securities.

title: Meeting with Trek Labs, Inc. and Barbour, Griffith & Rogers
author: Trek Labs, Inc. and Barbour, Griffith & Rogers
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met on April 21, 2026 with representatives of Trek Labs (Backpack) — Can Sun and Mark Wetjen — and Keaghan Ames of BGR Group to discuss crypto asset regulatory issues.
According to the attached agenda, Backpack planned to describe its business model, trading, custody, and risk management practices, and its prior regulatory engagement in other jurisdictions.
Backpack indicated it would outline its rationale for entering or re-entering the U.S. market and stated a commitment to operating within U.S. regulatory frameworks.

The agenda stated Backpack would update staff on its CFTC discussions regarding potential DCM and/or DCO registration and discuss how it is evaluating securities law implications of its products, including token listings.
takeaway: Backpack (Trek Labs) met with SEC staff to discuss its U.S. market entry plans, trading/custody practices, and parallel CFTC registration efforts.

title: Meeting with Blockaid, Inc. and Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher LLP
author: Blockaid, Inc. and Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher LLP
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met on April 20, 2026 with representatives of Blockaid, Inc. and Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher LLP to discuss approaches to regulating crypto assets, referencing an attached document.

In its February 24, 2026 meeting request, Blockaid cited Chairman Atkins's Project Crypto speech and argued that a fit-for-purpose digital asset framework should be paired with measures strengthening ecosystem security, investor protection, and market confidence.

Blockaid described itself as a provider of transaction-level threat prevention and simulation technology, and offered to brief staff on onchain security challenges, market-tested risk controls, and potential principles-based regulatory approaches.
Blockaid proposed attendees Ido Ben Natan (CEO), Roni Sidi (General Counsel), and Pete Eskew (VP Government), and referenced its prior written submission dated January 5, 2026.
takeaway: Blockaid pitched its onchain threat-prevention technology and urged pairing a digital-asset regulatory framework with stronger ecosystem security measures.

title: Meeting with Collab+Currency, as well as Students Arjun Yenamandra, Osian Guthrie, Hugo Okada, Jacob Handler, Rishab Rai, and Julia Ostroff
author: Collab+Currency, as well as Students Arjun Yenamandra, Osian Guthrie, Hugo Okada, Jacob Handler, Rishab Rai, and Julia Ostroff
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met on April 16, 2026 with a Collab+Currency representative and six university students to discuss approaches to crypto asset regulation, based on a document the participants provided.
Collab+Currency described itself as a seed-stage venture firm investing in decentralized technology and running a student engagement platform across university blockchain clubs.

The participants proposed student presentations on three topics: harmonizing U.S. stablecoin rules with global frameworks; crypto-backed mortgages via on-chain debt capital markets; and token networks as capital formation mechanisms.
The participants stated the session's goal was constructive dialogue to identify regulatory friction points and explore how Commission clarity could keep builders operating within U.S. jurisdiction.
takeaway: Collab+Currency and university students presented proposals on stablecoin harmonization, crypto-backed mortgages, and token-based capital formation to the Crypto Task Force.

title: Meeting with Alpaca Securities LLC and Lowenstein Sandler LLP
author: Alpaca Securities LLC and Lowenstein Sandler LLP
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met on April 14, 2026 with representatives of Alpaca Securities LLC and Lowenstein Sandler LLP to discuss approaches to regulating crypto assets, referencing a document Alpaca provided.

Alpaca described a proposed expansion into digital asset securities activities, including direct custody for customers and proprietary accounts and omnibus custody at a transfer agent treated as a good control location under Rule 15c3-3.

Alpaca stated it would apply controls such as MPC, HSMs, policy-based key governance, direct ledger access, contingency planning, and daily reconciliation to meet Rule 15c3-3(b)(1) possession requirements.

Alpaca argued that recent Division of Trading and Markets and Division of Investment Management guidance leaves a gap for clearing broker-dealers, and requested interpretive guidance, no-action relief, or other Commission action.
takeaway: Alpaca Securities seeks SEC relief to custody digital asset securities directly and via omnibus accounts under Rule 15c3-3 as a clearing broker-dealer.

title: Meeting with QCP Capital Pte. Ltd., Winston & Strawn LLP, and McIntyre & Lemon, PLLC
author: QCP Capital Pte. Ltd., Winston & Strawn LLP, and McIntyre & Lemon, PLLC
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met on April 7, 2026 with representatives of QCP Capital Pte. Ltd., Winston & Strawn LLP, and McIntyre & Lemon, PLLC.
According to the memorandum, the discussion addressed approaches to regulating crypto assets.
The memorandum notes that the participating representatives provided a document that was discussed during the meeting.
takeaway: QCP Capital and legal counsel met with the Crypto Task Force to discuss approaches to regulating crypto assets.

title: Meeting with Internet Capital Market Exchange, Inc., BakerHostetler LLP, Sidley Austin LLP, and C Guidry Law, PLLC
author: Internet Capital Market Exchange, Inc., BakerHostetler LLP, Sidley Austin LLP, and C Guidry Law, PLLC
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff, including Commissioner Peirce, met on April 6, 2026 with representatives of Internet Capital Market Exchange (ICME), BakerHostetler, Sidley Austin, and C Guidry Law to discuss crypto asset regulation.
ICME representatives requested exemptive relief for a proposed 'Innovation Program,' the specifics of which they asked to keep confidential.

According to the submission, the proposed program would draw on elements of Regulation Crowdfunding and Regulation A to make tokenized securities retail-accessible and liquid while reducing issuer costs.
takeaway: ICME sought SEC exemptive relief for a confidential program blending Reg CF and Reg A elements to offer liquid, retail-accessible tokenized securities.

title: Meeting with Figure Technology Solutions, Inc.
author: Figure Technology Solutions, Inc.
key_points: Figure Technology Solutions representative Mike Cagney met with Crypto Task Force staff on April 1, 2026 to discuss transfer agent requirements as applied to yielding (security) stablecoins, specifically YLDS.

Cagney stated that the SEC's position requiring transfer agents to obtain security holders' names and addresses effectively serves as a proxy for KYC enforcement, which he argued is outside the SEC's domain.
Cagney contended that a transfer agent's role is primarily for quorum purposes, for which a wallet address should suffice.
Cagney asserted that the SEC's current stance disadvantages security-version yielding stablecoins relative to GENIUS Act stablecoins, which he said should not be the agency's intent.
takeaway: Figure's CEO argued SEC transfer agent KYC requirements unfairly burden yielding security stablecoins like YLDS versus GENIUS Act stablecoins.

title: Meeting with Groovy Company, Inc.
author: Groovy Company, Inc.
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met with Groovy Company, Inc. (dba OTCM Protocol) on March 30, 2026, to discuss approaches to crypto asset regulation, per the meeting memorandum.

Groovy representatives described OTCM Protocol as a Solana-based tokenization platform for illiquid OTC/Expert/Grey Market securities, using SPL Token-2022 Transfer Hooks to enforce 42 compliance controls and Empire Stock Transfer as transfer agent.

The company argued its architecture maps to Category 1 Model B of the January 28, 2026 Joint Staff Statement on Tokenized Securities, with the transfer agent holding the authoritative record and blockchain serving as notification layer.

Groovy requested SEC guidance or no-action relief on five questions, including whether transfer agents can serve as qualified custodians for tokenized securities on permissionless blockchains and whether its CEDEX bonding-curve AMM requires exchange or ATS registration.
takeaway: Groovy Company sought SEC guidance on its Solana-based tokenization platform for illiquid OTC securities, requesting no-action relief on custody and exchange registration questions.

title: Meeting with Republic and C Guidry Law, PLLC
author: Republic and C Guidry Law, PLLC
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met on March 25, 2026 with representatives of Republic and C Guidry Law, PLLC to discuss approaches to crypto asset regulation, following a March 5 meeting with Commissioner Uyeda.
Republic expressed appreciation for the staff Statement on Tokenized Securities and the December DTC No-Action Letter, and sought to discuss potential expansions of that relief.
Republic indicated support for the Chairman's proposed Innovation Exemption and offered its perspective on tokenization developments in the EU and UK that it believes could be introduced in the U.S.
Republic requested additional staff guidance on secondary markets for private securities and asked that Corporation Finance's Office of Small Business Policy and Commissioner advisors be included.
takeaway: Republic sought expanded tokenized securities relief, secondary market guidance for private securities, and discussed international tokenization models.

title: Meeting with Ripple, Ripple Prime, and Katten Muchin Rosenman LLP
author: Ripple, Ripple Prime, and Katten Muchin Rosenman LLP
key_points: Ripple and Ripple Prime representatives met with Crypto Task Force staff on March 20, 2026 to discuss regulation of payment stablecoins and tokenized securities under broker-dealer net capital (15c3-1) and customer protection (15c3-3) rules.

Ripple's submission requested aligning payment stablecoin treatment as a cash substitute, referenced the February 19, 2026 Division of Trading and Markets FAQ applying a 2% haircut, and urged calibrating haircuts to short-dated U.S. government debt.

Ripple proposed permitting self-custody or custody at regulated digital asset custodians for firm assets, and extending to broker-dealers the custody relief available to 1940 Act funds for client assets held at federally chartered digital asset banks.

Ripple argued existing SEC rules should apply consistently to tokenized securities carrying equivalent risks, and asked whether clarity should come via industry-wide guidance or firm-specific no-action relief.
takeaway: Ripple urged the SEC to treat payment stablecoins as cash substitutes and align broker-dealer capital and custody rules for tokenized securities.

title: Meeting with InfoChat, Inc.
author: InfoChat, Inc.
key_points: InfoChat described itself as a non-custodial, on-chain social platform that routes trades to third-party DEXs and prediction market protocols (e.g., Hyperliquid, Polymarket), offers fiat on/off-ramps via a stablecoin provider, embedded debit cards, and yield/staking access.

InfoChat asked whether routing orders to third-party DEXs without matching or custody—while collecting builder fees—triggers broker-dealer registration, and what registration pathway exists for non-custodial on-chain platforms.

InfoChat requested the Commission's views on whether binary outcome tokens on third-party prediction markets constitute securities or security-based swaps, and whether front-end providers bear the same obligations as market operators.

InfoChat sought guidance on AI agents executing trades and transfers under human ownership, and asked about potential exemptions, no-action relief, or sandbox pathways for early-stage non-custodial platforms.
takeaway: InfoChat sought SEC guidance on whether its non-custodial order-routing social platform triggers broker-dealer registration and how prediction market tokens are classified.

title: Meeting with Yuma, Digital Currency Group, Inc., and Cooley LLP
author: Yuma, Digital Currency Group, Inc., and Cooley LLP
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met on March 9, 2026 with representatives of Yuma, Digital Currency Group, and Cooley LLP to discuss regulatory approaches to crypto assets.

Yuma requested staff views on whether a 'Subnet Token Staking Transaction'—a subnet operator acquiring TAO, swapping it for subnet tokens, and staking those tokens to a subnet validator on Bittensor—constitutes a securities transaction.

The submitted agenda indicated Yuma's representatives planned to outline a legal analysis arguing that Subnet Token Staking Transactions do not constitute securities transactions and to discuss the need for staff relief.
takeaway: Yuma and allies sought SEC staff views on whether Bittensor subnet token staking transactions qualify as securities transactions.

title: Meeting with Molecule AG and Cahill Gordon & Reindel LLP
author: Molecule AG and Cahill Gordon & Reindel LLP
key_points: Molecule AG and Cahill Gordon representatives met with Crypto Task Force staff on March 2, 2026 to present the 'Coin-to-Company' (C2C) model for funding decentralized science (DeSci) research.

Molecule's submission proposes a dual-entity structure—a Delaware LLC 'DevCo' issuing tokens and equity, and a Delaware Unincorporated Nonprofit Association DAO—asserting that utility tokens remain categorically separate from equity securities.

The presenters argued that project tokens, lacking economic rights or enforceable claims, fall outside Howey, and that voluntary token locking does not constitute an option under Gwozdzinsky v. Zell-Chilmark Fund.
Molecule requested Task Force views on token classification, Rule 701 use for community contributors, DAO liability under Samuels v. Lido DAO, and possible safe harbors or tailored DeSci guidance.
takeaway: Molecule AG proposed a dual-entity DeSci funding model arguing utility tokens fall outside Howey and sought Task Force guidance on classification and safe harbors.

title: Meeting with Superform Labs and Manatt, Phelps & Phillips LLP
author: Superform Labs and Manatt, Phelps & Phillips LLP
key_points: Superform Labs and Manatt, Phelps & Phillips representatives met with Crypto Task Force staff on February 17, 2026 to discuss regulatory approaches for non-custodial onchain yield infrastructure.

Superform's representatives requested no-action relief, exemptive relief, or rulemaking establishing a conditional safe harbor, describing their platform as a non-custodial interface that never holds user assets or executes transactions on users' behalf.

Superform proposed safe harbor conditions—including no custody, no discretionary execution, standardized risk and conflict disclosures, neutral interface design, and operator transparency—that would clarify non-applicability of broker-dealer, adviser, and Investment Company Act frameworks.

Superform's submission invoked Section 230 as an analogy for distinguishing access-providing platforms from intermediaries, while acknowledging anti-fraud provisions under Securities Act Section 17(a) and Exchange Act Rule 10b-5 would still apply.
takeaway: Superform Labs sought safe harbor or no-action relief to exempt non-custodial onchain yield interfaces from broker-dealer, adviser, and Investment Company Act requirements.

title: Meeting with Blue Gold Limited, Blockchain Intelligence Group, and Willkie Farr & Gallagher LLP
author: Blue Gold Limited, Blockchain Intelligence Group, and Willkie Farr & Gallagher LLP
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met on January 28, 2026 with representatives of Blue Gold Limited, Blockchain Intelligence Group, and Willkie Farr & Gallagher LLP to discuss crypto asset regulation approaches.
Blue Gold's meeting request proposed to describe the Blue Gold One Standard Gold Token, which is planned to be backed by a reserve of physical gold bullion.
Blue Gold's representatives indicated they would argue, applying the Howey test, that the token is a product rather than a security and does not constitute an investment contract.
The representatives also sought to discuss disclosure and reporting implications for Blue Gold as an SEC-registered public issuer that also issues a digital token.
takeaway: Blue Gold sought SEC guidance on whether its gold-bullion-backed token qualifies as a product rather than a security under the Howey test.

title: Meeting with Securities Industry and Financial Markets Association, Cahill Gordon & Reindel LLP, Citadel Securities, and JPMorgan Chase & Co.
author: Securities Industry and Financial Markets Association, Cahill Gordon & Reindel LLP, Citadel Securities, and JPMorgan Chase & Co.
key_points: SIFMA representatives, joined by Cahill Gordon & Reindel, Citadel Securities, and JPMorgan Chase, met with Crypto Task Force staff on January 27, 2026 to discuss four prior submissions on tokenized securities regulation.

SIFMA argued that tokenized securities should be subject to the same investor protection and market integrity principles as other securities, and that any exemptive relief should be narrow, economically grounded, and paired with notice-and-comment rulemaking.

SIFMA contended that DeFi models performing broker, dealer, or exchange functions should be regulated as such, and warned that large-scale peer-to-peer or peer-to-crowd securities trading outside federal securities laws would resemble pre-1930s unregulated markets.

SIFMA proposed a taxonomy distinguishing native tokenized securities, wrapped tokenized securities, and security entitlement tokens, and stated wallet providers conducting brokerage activities or holding customer keys should register as broker-dealers.
takeaway: SIFMA urged the SEC to regulate tokenized securities under existing frameworks, requiring broker-dealer registration for DeFi and wallet providers performing regulated functions.

title: Meeting with Bluprynt and Chainlink Labs
author: Bluprynt and Chainlink Labs
key_points: Bluprynt and Chainlink Labs representatives met with Crypto Task Force staff on January 23, 2026 to demonstrate Know-Your-Issuer (KYI), Bluprynt's Policy Engine, and Chainlink's Automated Compliance Engine (ACE) as tools for tokenized-market compliance.

The presenters argued that adoption of new compliance infrastructure is impeded by switching costs, incumbent reticence absent regulatory signals, reliance on traditional gatekeepers, and network effects, and urged including compliance technology in any SEC innovation exemption.

Referencing the DTCC/DTC tokenization no-action letter as constructive but potentially narrowing, Bluprynt asked that tokenization pilots preserve modular, competitive compliance innovation rather than harden centralized chokepoints.

The participants requested non-mandatory SEC staff guidance clarifying that Verified Wallet Identifiers, on-chain permissioned ledger records, and private-key possession with provenance attestations can satisfy Rules 17Ad-7/9/10, 15c3-3, and 17a-3/4.
takeaway: Bluprynt and Chainlink urged the SEC to issue guidance recognizing on-chain compliance tools for tokenized markets and preserve competitive innovation in exemption frameworks.

title: Meeting with Restored Law PLLC, Indiana House, and Megawatt
author: Restored Law PLLC, Indiana House, and Megawatt
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met on January 21, 2026 with representatives of Restored Law PLLC, Indiana House, and Megawatt to discuss approaches to crypto asset regulation.

The participants shared an introduced Indiana House bill (LS 6415) that would require certain state retirement and savings plans to offer cryptocurrency ETFs and permit other public funds to invest in crypto or stablecoin ETFs.

The bill would also establish a Blockchain and Digital Assets Task Force to recommend up to two pilot projects, and preempt local rules restricting digital asset payments, self-custody, mining, or noncustodial software.
The bill provides that noncustodial digital asset transfer software is not money transmission and that courts may compel disclosure of a private key only when no other means suffices.
takeaway: Indiana House bill LS 6415 would mandate crypto ETF options in state retirement plans and preempt local restrictions on digital asset activities.

title: Meeting with CompliLedger
author: CompliLedger
key_points: CompliLedger's representative, Founder and CCO Maranda Harris, met with Crypto Task Force staff on January 20, 2026 to discuss compliance automation for digital asset firms under Regulation S-P, Regulation S-ID, cybersecurity, and recordkeeping requirements.

The CompliLedger brief identified industry challenges it observed, including translating regulatory text into enforced technical controls, aligning policies with system behavior, demonstrating ongoing rather than point-in-time compliance, and balancing transparency with data protection.

CompliLedger stated that compliance automation should support rather than replace human judgment, maintain auditability, avoid 'checkbox compliance,' and use data minimization and least-privilege access to generate evidence without over-disclosing customer data.

CompliLedger stated the meeting sought SEC staff perspectives on deficiencies in automation approaches and appropriate guardrails, and did not seek legal advice, interpretive guidance, or regulatory relief.
takeaway: CompliLedger met with SEC Crypto Task Force to discuss compliance automation challenges for digital asset firms under existing regulatory requirements.

title: Meeting with Flow Traders U.S. LLC
author: Flow Traders U.S. LLC
key_points: Flow Traders U.S. LLC representatives met with Crypto Task Force staff on January 20, 2026, to discuss regulation of crypto assets, providing a document describing the firm as a proprietary liquidity provider active in digital assets.

Flow Traders requested discussion of broker-dealer activity on blockchain infrastructure, including trading tokenized securities for its own account, Rule 15c3-1 net capital considerations, custody, clearance, settlement, and use of permissionless blockchains for books and records.

Flow Traders also sought guidance on DeFi issues, including proprietary liquidity provision in decentralized pools, potential exchange/broker/dealer classification of protocol interactions, and extraterritorial application of U.S. securities laws.
takeaway: Flow Traders met with the Crypto Task Force to discuss broker-dealer rules for blockchain-based trading and DeFi liquidity provision.

title: Meeting with Finternet Global LLP and Bodin Advisory LLC
author: Finternet Global LLP and Bodin Advisory LLC
key_points: The submission from Finternet Global LLP and Bodin Advisory LLC responds to the SEC Crypto Task Force's tokenization roundtable, proposing universal technical standards and flow-based regulatory approaches informed by U.S., EU, and Singapore frameworks.
The authors advocate for technology-neutral baseline standards, including a Unified Interledger Protocol enabling interoperability across ledgers without presupposing an asset's legal classification.

The authors argue regulation should accommodate both self-custody and third-party custody, while recommending mandatory qualified custodians for institutional investors, tokenized securities settlements, and high-risk public interest contexts.

The authors recommend shifting from asset-classification to flow-based regulation with programmable compliance (e.g., ERC-3643, tokenized identity, Travel Rule-style controls), implemented via a phased framework and sandbox pilots.
takeaway: Finternet Global and Bodin Advisory propose universal interoperability standards and flow-based, programmable compliance as alternatives to asset-classification regulation for tokenized assets.

title: Meeting with Injective Labs, Inc., Injective, LLC, and Injective Foundation
author: Injective Labs, Inc., Injective, LLC, and Injective Foundation
key_points: Injective Labs, Injective LLC, and Injective Foundation representatives met with Crypto Task Force staff on January 13, 2026 to discuss crypto asset regulatory approaches, submitting a prior letter and comment exhibits.

The Injective representatives requested discussion of whether qualifying decentralized DeFi protocols should be excluded from 'exchange' and 'broker-dealer' definitions, and a safe harbor modeled on Commissioner Peirce's proposed Rule 195.

Injective sought exemptive relief for neutral DeFi frontend interfaces and confirmation that specified non-custodial, over-collateralized DeFi credit protocols do not involve Howey investment contracts or Reves notes.
Injective also proposed discussing compliance paths for tokenized real-world assets and tokenized equities, including transfer agent, broker-dealer, and ATS licensing options.
takeaway: Injective sought exclusion of decentralized protocols from exchange/broker-dealer definitions, safe harbor relief, and compliance paths for tokenized assets.

title: Meeting with Lumia and Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher LLP
author: Lumia and Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher LLP
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met on January 13, 2026 with representatives of Lumia (including Co-Founder/CTO Deniz Dalkilic and Head of Communications Mark Herndon) and Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher LLP to discuss approaches to crypto asset regulation.
Lumia's proposed agenda described its rwaUSD stablecoin design as aligned with GENIUS Act principles, citing reserve integrity, disclosures, redemption safeguards, and KYC-linked issuance.
Lumia outlined its Lumia Stream and L-Tokens for liquidity aggregation, and proposed zk-audit proofs and permission-aware architecture to reconcile compliance with investor privacy.

Lumia proposed collaboration through sandbox engagement using anonymized data and offered to serve as a case study for tokenized real estate, stablecoins, and credit products, and discussed AI applications in onboarding and tokenization.
takeaway: Lumia presented its rwaUSD stablecoin and tokenization infrastructure to the SEC Crypto Task Force, proposing sandbox collaboration and compliance-oriented architecture.

title: Meeting with USIG Digital
author: USIG Digital
key_points: Crypto Task Force staff met with representatives of USIG Digital on January 12, 2026 to discuss approaches to regulating crypto assets.
USIG Digital provided documents during the meeting, which primarily introduced the company's leadership team, advisors, and their backgrounds in capital markets and digital assets.

The materials indicate USIG's stated mission focuses on modernizing on-chain collateral mobility, with team experience spanning tokenized Treasuries, real-world asset structuring, and the Canton Network.
takeaway: USIG Digital met with Crypto Task Force staff to discuss crypto regulation, highlighting its focus on on-chain collateral mobility and real-world asset tokenization.

title: Base Case: Remarks at the IC3 Blockchain Camp
author: Hester M. Peirce
key_points: Commissioner Peirce urged the crypto industry to address problems such as DeFi hacks through technical and self-regulatory measures rather than waiting for legal fixes, citing steps like code audits, delays, and key-management standards.

Peirce outlined guiding principles: publishing open-source code is First Amendment-protected speech; blockchains are general-purpose infrastructure outside the SEC's remit; and neutral infrastructure providers should not be treated as securities market participants.

She distinguished 'genuine DeFi' from onchain CeFi, stating that centralized actors with discretion or custody may warrant securities regulation, while non-custodial software tools enabling peer transactions should not.

Peirce noted the SEC is proceeding cautiously, referencing the April 2026 staff statement on user interfaces and a planned innovation exemption for onchain NMS stock trading, and called for reconsidering exchange and broker definitions.
takeaway: Commissioner Peirce argues genuine DeFi protocols are not securities intermediaries and urges industry self-regulation alongside narrowly tailored SEC oversight.

title: Keynote Remarks at The Economic Club of Washington
author: Paul S. Atkins
key_points: Chairman Atkins outlined an 'A-C-T' strategy to advance modernized rules, clarify jurisdiction, and transform the SEC rulebook, describing it as a course correction from the prior administration's approach.

On digital assets, Atkins stated the SEC launched Project Crypto, published a crypto-token taxonomy classifying five categories (four deemed non-securities), and is preparing an 'innovation exemption' for compliant on-chain trading of tokenized securities.
Atkins reported that he and CFTC Chairman Mike Selig signed an MOU aligning definitions, clarifying jurisdiction, and coordinating oversight in shared areas including digital assets.
Atkins described a 'Make IPOs Great Again' agenda, directing staff to evaluate an IPO on-ramp, broader smaller-company accommodations, easier shelf registration, and optional semiannual reporting.
takeaway: Chairman Atkins unveiled a broad SEC modernization agenda including a crypto-token taxonomy, innovation exemption for tokenized securities, and SEC-CFTC jurisdictional MOU.

title: Interfacing With Our Inner Demons: Comments on the Division of Trading and Markets' Statement on Certain User Interfaces
author: Hester M. Peirce
key_points: Commissioner Peirce commended the Division of Trading and Markets' statement describing circumstances in which staff will not object to interface providers for self-custodial wallets operating without broker-dealer registration under Exchange Act Section 15(b).

Peirce stated she prefers a more permanent regulatory approach over staff statements, arguing existing law already indicates that wallets and interfaces enabling self-custody, data viewing, or message formatting are not brokers.
Peirce criticized what she characterized as the SEC's history of expansively interpreting 'broker' through no-action letters and enforcement actions, citing her prior Neovest dissent.
Peirce solicited public feedback to inform future rulemaking reassessing terms like 'broker' in light of new technologies.
takeaway: Commissioner Peirce supports permanent rules clarifying that self-custodial wallet interfaces are not brokers, rather than relying on staff no-action statements.

title: Opening Remarks at the Digital Asset Summit 2026
author: Paul S. Atkins
key_points: Chairman Atkins stated that the SEC last week published a token taxonomy and interpretation of Howey intended to clarify when a crypto asset implicates the federal securities laws.
According to Atkins, the framework identifies five categories of digital assets, four of which are not securities, and begins outlining compliance paths for crypto fundraises.
Atkins characterized the interpretation as a foundation rather than an endpoint, saying only Congress can 'future-proof' regulation through comprehensive market structure legislation.
Atkins noted the remarks reflect his own views, not necessarily those of the SEC or other Commissioners.
takeaway: Chairman Atkins announced a new SEC token taxonomy clarifying most digital asset categories are not securities, while urging Congress to legislate comprehensive market structure.

title: SEC Clarifies the Application of Federal Securities Laws to Crypto Assets
author: Press Release
key_points: The SEC issued an interpretation clarifying how federal securities laws apply to certain crypto assets and transactions, with the CFTC joining to administer the Commodity Exchange Act consistently.

Chairman Atkins stated that most crypto assets are not themselves securities and that investment contracts can come to an end, describing the interpretation as a bridge pending Congressional market structure legislation.

According to the release, the interpretation provides a token taxonomy (digital commodities, collectibles, tools, stablecoins, and securities) and addresses when non-security crypto assets become or cease being subject to investment contracts.
The interpretation also clarifies how federal securities laws apply to airdrops, protocol mining, protocol staking, and wrapping of non-security crypto assets.
takeaway: SEC issued a joint interpretation with the CFTC creating a token taxonomy and clarifying when crypto assets are subject to securities laws, pending Congressional legislation.

title: Remarks at the Investor Advisory Committee Meeting
author: Paul S. Atkins
key_points: Chairman Atkins stated he expects the Commission to soon consider a time- and scope-limited innovation exemption to facilitate limited trading of certain tokenized securities while a longer-term framework is developed.

Atkins thanked the Investor Advisory Committee for engaging with tokenization of equity securities and acknowledged its recognition that tokenization can enhance settlement efficiency, reduce settlement risk, and eliminate unnecessary intermediaries.
Atkins noted the Crypto Task Force has, over thirteen months, held roundtables, met with hundreds of market participants, and received written input to inform calibration of rules for novel trading.
Atkins said the Commission continues to welcome public comment on the design of the potential innovation exemption.
takeaway: Chairman Atkins signals the SEC will soon consider a time-limited innovation exemption enabling trading of certain tokenized securities.

title: Adam’s Lib: Remarks at the Meeting of the SEC Investor Advisory Committee
author: Hester M. Peirce
key_points: Commissioner Peirce invoked Adam Smith's classical liberalism to urge regulatory humility and welcomed the Investor Advisory Committee's input on paring back public company disclosure requirements she views as potentially obfuscating.

Peirce stated that fund proxy quorum requirements under the Investment Company Act are burdensome, and emphasized that proxy votes belong to the fund, not individual shareholders, obligating advisers to vote in the fund's interest.
Peirce noted SEC staff is developing a narrow innovation exemption for limited trading of certain tokenized securities, narrower than the 'blanket' exemption in the Committee's draft recommendation.

Peirce posed questions to the Committee on tokenization, including sufficiency of existing disclosures, necessity of T+1 reforms for atomic settlement, regulation where intermediaries are absent or undefined, issuer consent, and safeguards against regulatory arbitrage.
takeaway: Commissioner Peirce signals SEC staff is crafting a narrow tokenized-securities trading exemption while posing key policy questions to the Investor Advisory Committee.

title: Remarks for the Investor Advisory Committee Meeting
author: Mark T. Uyeda
key_points: Commissioner Uyeda thanked departing Investor Advisory Committee members and previewed panels on Regulation S-K disclosure reform and fund proxy voting, noting staff have begun a comprehensive review of S-K requirements.
Uyeda stated that fund proxy voting faces quorum challenges and rising costs borne by shareholders, and invited Committee ideas on modernizing the framework and the SEC's role.

Uyeda said the Committee will consider a draft recommendation on tokenization of equity securities, comparing it to prior innovations like money market funds and ETFs that initially required exemptive relief before codification.
Uyeda suggested tokenization may not fit neatly within the existing regulatory framework and that rules may need to evolve while preserving investor protection and fair, orderly, efficient markets.
takeaway: Commissioner Uyeda previewed an Investor Advisory Committee recommendation on equity tokenization, suggesting existing rules may need to evolve for the technology.

title: SEC and CFTC Announce Historic Memorandum of Understanding Between Agencies
author: Press Release
key_points: The SEC and CFTC announced an MOU on March 11, 2026 to coordinate oversight, align regulatory definitions, and enable data sharing, which the agencies said aims to support innovation, market integrity, and investor protection.
SEC Chairman Atkins stated the MOU provides a roadmap to end regulatory turf wars and duplicative registrations; CFTC Chairman Selig said it would harmonize frameworks and close regulatory gaps.

The agencies launched a Joint Harmonization Initiative, co-led by Robert Teply (SEC) and Meghan Tente (CFTC), covering product definitions, clearing and margin, dual registrants, crypto assets, reporting, and enforcement coordination.
The agencies invited public input via a written input form or meeting request.
takeaway: SEC and CFTC signed a landmark MOU to coordinate crypto oversight, harmonize definitions, share data, and eliminate duplicative regulation.

title: Cutting by Two Would Do
author: Hester M. Peirce
key_points: Commissioner Peirce noted that the SEC's Division of Trading and Markets issued an FAQ stating staff would not object to broker-dealers applying a 2% haircut on proprietary payment stablecoin positions under Exchange Act Rule 15c3-1.

Peirce expressed support for the staff's approach, stating stablecoins are essential to blockchain-based transactions and would enable broker-dealers to expand activities involving tokenized securities and other crypto assets.

Peirce said a 100% haircut, which some broker-dealers had proposed out of caution, would be unnecessarily punitive, noting the 2% figure aligns with the haircut applied to money market funds holding similar reserve instruments.

Peirce indicated interest in Commission-level amendments to Rule 15c3-1 addressing payment stablecoins and invited market participants to share views on other rules warranting modification for SEC-registered entities' stablecoin use.
takeaway: Commissioner Peirce supports a 2% net capital haircut for broker-dealer stablecoin positions, replacing an overly punitive 100% haircut.

title: Number Go Down and Other Schadenfreude - Remarks at ETHDenver
author: Paul S. Atkins, Hester M. Peirce
key_points: Chairman Atkins and Commissioner Peirce reviewed the SEC's past-year crypto actions, including ending regulation by enforcement, issuing staff guidance on mining, staking, meme coins and stablecoins, rescinding SAB 121, approving generic listing standards for crypto ETPs, and launching a joint CFTC-SEC Project Crypto initiative.

Atkins previewed forthcoming SEC work, including a Commission framework on investment-contract analysis for crypto assets, a rulemaking on broker-dealer custody of non-security crypto assets including payment stablecoins, transfer agent modernization, and capital-raising pathways for crypto asset sales.

Atkins described a contemplated innovation exemption that would permit limited, white-listed trading of tokenized securities on AMMs and decentralized platforms, temporarily and with possible relief from certain rules, while longer-term rules are developed; Peirce cautioned expectations should be moderated.

Both stated regulators should not react to falling crypto prices; Peirce said regulatory clarity helps but value comes from building useful products, and expressed interest in enabling rights-bearing tokens that fall within the securities framework.
takeaway: Atkins and Peirce outlined the SEC's sweeping crypto reform agenda, previewing frameworks on security status, custody, capital raising, and a possible innovation exemption for tokenized securities on DeFi platforms.

title: Opening Remarks at Joint SEC-CFTC Harmonization Event – Project Crypto
author: Paul S. Atkins
key_points: Chairman Atkins announced that Project Crypto will proceed as a joint SEC-CFTC initiative, launched alongside CFTC Chairman Mike Selig at CFTC headquarters, aimed at harmonizing crypto asset market regulation across both agencies.

Atkins stated that SEC staff in 2025 issued more crypto-related clarifications than in the prior decade, citing guidance from Corporation Finance on memecoins, stablecoins, mining, and staking, and from Trading and Markets and Investment Management divisions.

Atkins argued that fragmented regulation across integrated markets creates investor confusion, and said the agencies will work to reduce friction and harmonize standards while preparing to implement pending bipartisan market structure legislation.
Atkins credited Commissioner Peirce and Selig with restoring innovator confidence in engaging the SEC, and described Selig's CFTC approach as disciplined but open to emerging market structures.
takeaway: SEC and CFTC launched Project Crypto, a joint initiative to harmonize crypto asset regulation and reduce cross-agency fragmentation.

title: Re SEC Tokenization Framework Should Favor Issuer-Sponsored Tokens
author: Hinza Asif, Asia Web3 Alliance Japan (AWAJ) - Association
takeaway: AWAJ proposes Japan–U.S. regulatory coordination on tokenized securities via a bilateral sandbox bridge and formal roundtable.
key_points: Japan's reclassification of crypto as financial instruments under securities law creates a basis for regulatory convergence with the U.S.
Proposal recommends a bilateral Sandbox Bridge Board for mutual recognition and streamlined cross-border intake of sandbox-admitted products.
Calls for a formal Japan–U.S. roundtable covering tokenized securities, cross-border custody and trading, stablecoins, and investor protection.

title: Written Input Ahead of the Anticipated Crypto Assets Rulemaking (RIN 3235-AN38) — Secondary-Market Disclosure Infrastructure and Qualified Disclosure Publisher Recognition
author: Sherwood Neiss, GUARDD, Inc.
key_points: The CLARITY Act addresses asset classification but fails to resolve secondary trading barriers for crypto assets, leaving legal assets impractical to sell due to outdated information requirements and inconsistent state exemptions.

GUARDD proposes SEC recognition of Qualified Disclosure Publishers (QDPs) to enable compliant, standardized, and accessible issuer disclosures, facilitating lawful secondary trading and preempting state manual-exemption patchworks while preserving anti-fraud authority.

The submission urges the SEC to include secondary trading in its crypto assets rulemaking, establish QDP criteria, and standardize token-specific disclosures to ensure tradability and investor protection.

title: Re: Proposal to Establish the First U.S.– Japan Crypto Regulatory Sandbox Bridge and Request for the First Japan– U.S. Crypto Regulatory Roundtable in Washington, D.C. in 2026
author: Hinza Asif, Asia Web3 Alliance Japan (AWAJ) - Association
key_points: Japan’s House of Representatives has reclassified crypto assets as financial instruments under the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act, aligning Japanese law with a securities-law model and facilitating regulatory convergence with the U.S.

The proposal recommends establishing a bilateral “Sandbox Bridge Board” to enable startups admitted to a regulatory sandbox, exemptive, or no-action pathway in one jurisdiction to receive streamlined intake and coordinated supervision when testing the same product in the other, with mutual-recognition principles to reduce duplicative compliance.

The proposal requests a formal Japan–U.S. Crypto Regulatory Roundtable in Washington, D.C., to address classification and treatment of tokenized securities and real-world assets, cross-border trading and custody, stablecoin and payments regulation, and investor-protection standards for cross-border offerings.

title: Re: SEC Tokenization Framework Should Favor Issuer-Sponsored Tokens
author: Peter Duggan, President, Securities Transfer Association
key_points: The SEC should distinguish Issuer-Sponsored Tokens from Third-Party Tokens, ensuring only issuer-authorized, natively issued tokenized securities receive regulatory relief or exemptions.

Issuer authorization, clear disclosures, and robust transfer controls must be threshold requirements for any tokenized-securities framework, with Third-Party Tokens subject to prominent differentiation and compliance procedures.

The Commission should modernize the Direct Registration System (DRS) to facilitate efficient conversion between traditional and tokenized securities, preventing infrastructure bottlenecks that disadvantage Issuer-Sponsored Tokens.

title: Meeting with Hyperliquid Policy Center, XYZ Ltd., and Sullivan & Cromwell LLP
author: Hyperliquid Policy Center, XYZ Ltd., and Sullivan & Cromwell LLP
takeaway: Hyperliquid and counsel met with the SEC Task Force to discuss protocol technology and regulatory approaches.
key_points: Hyperliquid Policy Center, XYZ Ltd., and Sullivan & Cromwell LLP requested a meeting to discuss the Hyperliquid protocol, its technology, and ecosystem participants.
The participants aimed to provide an overview of the Hyperliquid protocol to the SEC Crypto Task Force regarding approaches to crypto asset regulation.

title: Meeting with Lockton Companies LLP and BGR Group LLC
author: Lockton Companies LLP and BGR Group LLC
takeaway: Lockton Companies provides specialized insurance brokerage to offer market clarity and risk management within the digital asset sector.
key_points: Lockton representatives stated that insurance serves as a mechanism for providing market clarity in the absence of regulatory certainty within the digital asset space.
Lockton's LEAP team provides specialized insurance brokerage and claims expertise for companies operating within the blockchain and digital asset sectors globally.

title: Re: No-Action Letter Relating to The Depository Trust Company’s Development of the DTCC Tokenization Services (Dec. 11, 2025); the Securities Transfer Association’s Comment Letter of July 1, 2026; and Recommendations for a Coherent, Investor-Protective...
author: Dave Hendricks, Vertalo
key_points: The issuer-sponsored model for tokenized securities, which preserves issuer control and authoritative ownership records, should be the foundation of the regulatory framework; modernization of DRS and FAST is a necessary precondition for fair competition among transfer agents.

The regulatory framework must recognize and address third-party tokenized products (including custodial and synthetic tokens) already in use, bringing them under clear investor-protective rules rather than driving them offshore.

Consistent application of the SEC’s January 2026 taxonomy is required, including to DTC’s tokenized entitlements, and relief should not be granted based on incumbency but on investor protection; distributed ledger technology should be considered as the long-term standard for securities ownership records.

title: Meeting with Aztec Laboratorium Limited
author: Aztec Laboratorium Limited
takeaway: Aztec Labs argues zero-knowledge proofs can fulfill KYC/AML obligations while enhancing privacy by avoiding centralized sensitive data storage.
key_points: Aztec Labs representatives argued that zero-knowledge proofs allow businesses to meet KYC and AML obligations by verifying specific attributes without collecting or storing sensitive personal data.
The company stated their ZKPassport technology enables identity, sanctions, and jurisdiction checks via on-device processing, avoiding the creation of centralized data 'honeypots'.
The representatives suggested zero-knowledge proofs could satisfy regulatory requirements for Sybil resistance, unique personhood, and verifiable record-keeping without requiring raw data possession.
